findings.description: Off-by-one pagination: offset = page * page_size with a 1-based page skips the entire first page (page 1 starts at offset page_size); should be (page - 1) * page_size.
Fetches all matching rows into memory and paginates/sorts in Python instead of using SQL LIMIT/OFFSET/ORDER BY, which does not scale with table size.
User-supplied name_pattern is compiled and run as a regex without validation, exposing the endpoint to catastrophic-backtracking ReDoS denial of service.
An invalid user-supplied regex raises an uncaught re.error, crashing the search request instead of returning a handled error.
A NULL display_name yields None, so matcher.search(None) (line 60) and u['display_name'].lower() (line 62) raise TypeError.
page_size is clamped only at the upper bound; zero or negative page_size is not guarded and yields empty or reversed slicing.
page is not validated, so page=0 or negative page produces a zero/negative offset and incorrect slicing (negative offset slices from the end).
DISTINCT department can include a NULL row, returning None in a list typed as list[str] and breaking str-expecting consumers.
deactivate commits but has no exception handling/rollback; a failure between execute and commit leaves the connection without an explicit rollback (mitigated by close).
re.compile runs on every call with no cache; repeated identical patterns recompile unnecessarily.
A fresh sqlite3 connection is opened per call with no reuse/pooling and no check_same_thread/timeout configuration.
